metadata.file_name: 103. Scherer et al. (2024). The potential of international large‑scale assessments for meta‑analyses in education.pdf
metadata.title: The potential of international large-scale assessments for meta-analyses in education

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: This is a methodological review of how meta-analysts can include international large-scale assessment (ILSA) data in education meta-analyses. The paper does not conduct an original student-level predictive modeling analysis on an ILSA micro-dataset; instead, it synthesizes and illustrates inclusion approaches (separate, indirect Bayesian, one-stage direct, two-stage direct) and reviews existing meta-analyses. Because no analytic sample of students is modeled with ML, there is no application of ILSA complex survey weights (e.g., student/school weights) or replicate-weight variance estimation (e.g., BRR/jackknife) in the sense required by the schema. Any discussion of complex survey design, plausible values, and replicate methods is conceptual guidance for effect-size extraction in primary studies rather than an implemented weighting strategy in this review.

data.handling_not_reported_explanation: This is a systematic methodological review and does not estimate student-level cognitive outcomes from ILSA plausible values (PVs) or report an implemented missing-data strategy for PVs. The paper discusses PV-based achievement estimation and complex survey design as considerations for effect-size computation in primary ILSA analyses, but it does not run an empirical PV-based model on an ILSA micro-dataset within the review itself. Therefore, PV handling and missing-data handling are not applicable as implemented analysis steps in this article.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: The study is a systematic review of existing education meta-analyses. It does not define an analytic student-level subsample from ILSA micro-data; instead, it applies screening and eligibility criteria to meta-analyses published in English (1995–2020) and restricts inclusion to correlational, observational research questions on gender differences in achievement and the relation between socioeconomic status (SES) and achievement.

main_findings.dataset_used: ILSA literature synthesis (systematic review; no single student-level analytic micro-dataset)
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using ILSA literature synthesis (systematic review; no single student-level analytic micro-dataset) the study leveraged the reported predictors to predict Literature synthesis outcome (not student-level prediction), finding that This methodological review synthesizes four approaches for integrating ILSA data into education meta-analyses: separate meta-analyses, indirect inclusion via Bayesian meta-analysis, one-stage direct inclusion, and two-stage direct inclusion. It also reports the status of ILSA inclusion in existing meta-analyses on gender differences in student achievement (m=8 meta-analyses; 448 primary studies; 6428 effect sizes) and on the relation between SES and student achievement (m=10 meta-analyses; 556 studies; 1631 effect sizes). The review finds that ILSA data are not eligible for all meta-analyses and that inclusion is limited, with few meta-analyses explicitly addressing dependence structures (e.g., multilevel meta-analysis). The paper emphasizes that correct effect-size extraction from complex survey designs (including weighting, clustering, and PV-based achievement estimation) and careful handling of nested meta-analytic data structures are key challenges for meta-analysts. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: This methodological review synthesizes four approaches for integrating ILSA data into education meta-analyses: separate meta-analyses, indirect inclusion via Bayesian meta-analysis, one-stage direct inclusion, and two-stage direct inclusion. It also reports the status of ILSA inclusion in existing meta-analyses on gender differences in student achievement (m=8 meta-analyses; 448 primary studies; 6428 effect sizes) and on the relation between SES and student achievement (m=10 meta-analyses; 556 studies; 1631 effect sizes). The review finds that ILSA data are not eligible for all meta-analyses and that inclusion is limited, with few meta-analyses explicitly addressing dependence structures (e.g., multilevel meta-analysis). The paper emphasizes that correct effect-size extraction from complex survey designs (including weighting, clustering, and PV-based achievement estimation) and careful handling of nested meta-analytic data structures are key challenges for meta-analysts.
data.null_fields_interpretation: This article is a methodological systematic review rather than an empirical ML study on ILSA micro-data. It does not train or evaluate machine-learning models, does not report an analytic student sample with per-country Ns, and does not implement PV-based achievement estimation or missing-data handling as part of an original analysis. Consequently, fields such as total_students, countries, and ML techniques are not applicable in the sense required for an ILSA predictive modeling extraction.
